risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomized to one of the 2 study arms using a computer-generated assignment. The computer-generated randomization result was sent to the study coordinator via email before the scheduled intervention session." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence, which is a truly random method and therefore supports low risk of bias for sequence generation.

Quote: "The computer-generated randomization result was sent to the study coordinator via email before the scheduled intervention session." Comment: The report indicates that allocation was generated centrally and transmitted to the study coordinator only after randomization, which would prevent the recruiter/enroller from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment. Although the paper does not use the phrase "allocation concealment," the described process is consistent with a mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Owing to the nature of the intervention, participants could not have been blinded to the study arm to which they were randomized. The orthopedic surgeons were blinded to patient randomization." Comment: Participants were explicitly not blinded, and although surgeons were blinded, this domain concerns participants and personnel delivering the intervention. Because the intervention was a decision aid versus an educational booklet, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect the main outcomes (recommendation for TKR and receipt of TKR), so the study is at high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "Research staff who were not involved in the intervention and were blinded to the study arm abstracted this information from the medical record." Comment: The outcomes were chart abstracted from the medical record by independent research staff who were blinded to allocation. The main outcomes (receipt of TKR and recommendation for surgery as recorded in records) are objective record-based outcomes, so knowledge of allocation would be unlikely to influence their measurement.

Quote: "Four participants withdrew from the study after baseline assessments. Of the remaining 336 participants ... 168 were randomized to the intervention (decision aid) arm and 168 to the control arm. ... Of those assigned to the control arm, 14 were unavailable for telephone follow-up. However, information on key clinical outcomes for all participants was abstracted from the medical record for full analysis" Comment: Although there was some loss to follow-up/engagement, the trial reports complete outcome data for the key clinical endpoints for all randomized participants, and the authors state that they had "complete data for all study participants". This makes attrition unlikely to have biased the results.
